data.title: Fifth Third Bancorp Series I: Attractively Balanced Risk Reward With This 8% Yielder
data.description:
Thesis

Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) is a bank out of Cincinnati, Ohio. Fifth Third Bancorp was founded in 1858, and it currently has a market capitalization of $30 billion, while its balance sheet has assets of over $200 billion.

Just like any bank, FITB employs a complex funding program, which includes debt, equity, and preferred equity. In today's article, we are going to focus on the Series I Preferred Shares (FITBI).

Balance sheet and capital snapshot

Firstly, Fifth Third Bancorp is considered a regional bank, serving customers within a specific region in the US. The bank is headquartered in Cincinnati and has a significant presence across the Midwest and Southeast United States, with a network of branches in states like Ohio, Kentucky, Indiana, Michigan, Illinois, etc.

As per the Seeking Alpha Financials tab, the bank has a balance sheet of over $200 billion, with over $160 billion in deposits. $42 billion of the deposit figure is represented by non-interest-bearing deposits. Fifth Third is rated investment grade by all major rating agencies:

A high rating is important when accessing cheap funding, although, via their leveraged nature, banks tend to move quickly from solvent to insolvent, as seen in the 2023 regional banking crisis.

When looking at a bank, it is important to assess its standing via capital ratios and, more importantly, the historic performance of such ratios. A well-run bank will have consistently high capital ratios, with only small occasional 'blips':

FITB has been fairly consistent at maintaining a CET-1 ratio of roughly 10% in the past years, with the minimum regulatory ratio at 4.5%.

Also, please note that because the bank has more than $100 billion in assets, it is subject to increased regulatory scrutiny as per the enhanced prudential standards:

The FRB’s capital plan rule requires BHCs with $100 billion or more in consolidated assets, including the Bancorp, to develop and maintain a capital plan approved by the Board of Directors on an annual basis. The mandatory elements of the capital plan are an assessment of the expected uses and sources of capital over a nine-quarter planning horizon, a description of all planned capital actions over the planning horizon, a discussion of any expected changes to the BHC’s business plan that are likely to have a material impact on its capital adequacy or liquidity, a detailed description of the BHC’s process for assessing capital adequacy and the BHC’s capital policy. (excerpt from the company's 2024 Annual Report)

Increased regulatory scrutiny means shareholders have additional checks and balances in respect to the risk run by the bank and its risk mitigation strategies. Please note that the Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act (EGRRCPA) of 2018 exempted most banks with assets between $50 billion and $100 billion from the most stringent enhanced prudential standards but did not impact FITB due to its size.

Tricolor subprime lender event

Just like any bank, Fifth Third is a lending institution and thus subject to the occasional large loss due to unforeseen circumstances. A notable recent development is the default of auto lender Tricolor:

(Bloomberg - Sept 10, 2025) -- JPMorgan Chase & Co., Fifth Third Bancorp, and Barclays Plc are among banks bracing for potentially hundreds of millions of dollars in combined losses from loans tied to subprime auto lender Tricolor Holdings, according to people with knowledge of the matter.

Fifth Third said in a regulatory filing Tuesday that it faced an impairment charge of as much as $200 million after discovering alleged fraudulent activity at a commercial borrower, which it didn’t identify. The bank said it was working with law enforcement authorities.

Sudden impairments like the $200 million mentioned above result in lower profitability and a lower capital ratio, all else equal. We expect the lending institution to continue to set capital aside to have a ratio in excess of 10%, but it is important to note the importance of the CET-1 ratio in absorbing unforeseen losses.

Capital markets performance

Let us also look at how the capital markets have treated FITB in the past few years, since this is a good snapshot into what the market thinks regarding the quality of the assets and the institution as a whole:

We are comparing the institution to the Invesco KBW Bank ETF (KBWB), the regional powerhouse M&T Bank Corp. (MTB), and the gold standard for megabanks, namely JPMorgan (JPM). FITB has done very well in the past five years, beating KBWB and MTB. Furthermore, its drawdown in 2023 was in line with what we saw for the regional ETF and MTB. The market has a neutral to favorable view of the institution when it comes to pricing its equity.

Preferred shares

As part of its capital and buffers, the bank has issued preferred equity. As we saw from the regional banks crisis, a bank run usually ends up in a default and a complete wipeout of common equity, preferred equity, and most of the senior bonds as well. We wrote two distinct articles on this topic:

  • First Republic: When Preferred Equity Goes To Zero, Part 2
  • Signature Bank: When Preferred Equity Goes To Zero

Make no mistake about it, if there is a significant credit event in respect to FITB, expect the preferred equity to potentially be wiped out in conjunction with the common equity.

That being said, we have a number of issuances from FITB:

FITBI is a fixed-to-floating issuance that now resets to a floating rate of 3-month SOFR plus 0.261% plus 3.71%. The initial call date was in December 2023. While the current yield is high at 8.17%, we do not think these securities will get called.

Call risk and yield considerations

As stated in the above section, the current yield is 8.17%, which is on the high side. However, please note that the securities are floating rate, and the forward curve indicates lower Fed Funds and SOFR. Simply put, there is a 1-to-1 correlation to Fed Funds here. A decrease of 100 bps in Fed Funds will see SOFR lower by 100 bps and FITBI's yield decrease proportionately.

In fact, many banks like floating-rate debt because many of their assets are floating, and they do not need to do any additional asset-liability matching. We are of the opinion that FITBI will not be called unless there is a significant increase in the credit rating for FITB or a merger that will see a much larger institution that can issue preferred equity cheaply.

While we do not think the call risk is there, holders will still be subject to downward pressure from a recession or another banking crisis:

The 2023 regional banking crisis saw a -15% drawdown in the securities, while all other recent events only saw -5% drawdowns.

Conclusion

FITBI represents preferred securities from Fifth Third and is perpetual in nature. The current yield is high at 8.17% given the investment grade rating for the institution, but the shares are floating rate. As Fed Funds move lower, FITBI's yield will also decrease. We do not think the securities will be called and think the risk/rewards are balanced here; hence, a 'Hold' rating.

data.title: SCHD: Enjoy The Dividends As Trumponomics Plays Out
data.description:
SCHD ETF: Relative underperformance likely to continue

I last covered the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (NYSEARCA:SCHD) on Aug. 1 with an article titled “SCHD Vs. Federal Reserve: My Notes On Dividends And Rates.” That article was motivated by the Fed’s rate decision at that time and rated SCHD as a hold.

Since then, there have been a few material developments in terms of macroeconomics (like the Fed’s updated dot plot) and also geopolitics. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the new developments of President Donald Trump’s economic policies. In particular, I will present my thoughts on the administration’s broad protective tariffs and the potential extension of corporate tax cuts as reflected in the legislative package known as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act." I will explain why these developments could have a conflicting impact on SCHD, thus clouding the return prospects of the ETF and likely to cause continued underperformance relative to the broader market.

SCHD ETF: Introduction and exposure

In case there are readers new to SCHD, it is one of the most popular ETFs among dividend investors – and for very good reasons. The top reasons on my list are Schwab’s brand name, rock-bottom fees, and its sound screening method for its holdings, as quote below.

SCHD Fund Description: SCHD’s index focuses on the sustainability and quality of dividends by screening companies based on four dividend quality attributes. These screens have provided enhanced yield and quality factors to the portfolio, along with distinct sector exposures.

As a result of its above screening methodology, the ETF's top holdings are drastically different from the top names in the broader market, which is heavily dominated by tech mega caps. As seen in the chart below, SCHD’s top holdings are better diversified across various non-tech sectors. Leading the list is Chevron (CVX), which makes up 4.38% of the fund. This is followed by AbbVie (ABBV) (healthcare), Altria Group (MO) (consumer staple), Home Depot (HD) (consumer discretionary), etc. Like the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPY), the fund is also overall top-heavy with its top 10 holdings collectively accounting for a substantial 41.19% of its total asset.

SCHD ETF: Potential impacts from Trumponomics

Given its indexing strategy, SCHD has vastly different sector weightings from the SP500 (as approximately by the SPY ETF), as shown in the next two charts. The most significant difference is in the Technology sector, as just mentioned, where SPY has a substantial 35.19% exposure compared to SCHD's 9.89%. Conversely, SCHD is heavily weighted toward Energy and Consumer Staple, both with about 19% allocation. In contrast, SPY has a minimal exposure of just 2.95% toward the energy sector and about 5% toward the consumer staple sector.

Given SCHD’s particular exposure, I see mixed impacts from the latest policy developments from the Trump administration. On the one hand, the administration kept pushing for broad protective tariffs. The administration has announced reciprocal tariffs on a wide range of goods from various countries since President Trump took office. As two latest developments since my last writing, the administration also imposed a 50% tariff on goods from India, and the president is lobbying EU for 100% tariffs on China and India. These issues could increase the input costs and compress profit margins for U.S. companies. Of course, the pressure will be felt by the overall economy. But I fear companies that make and sell physical goods (like consumer staple companies) will feel more pressure than companies that make and sell digital products.

On the other hand, the administration also kept making progress on the tax front. A key milestone since my last writing on this front is the legislative package known as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act." This bill made permanent many of the individual and business tax provisions from the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, which would boost after-tax profits for many of SCHD’s companies.

Other risks and final thoughts

Of course, at this point, many of you must be wondering about at least two questions. Won’t the tax cut benefit all companies in the economy, and why do I single out SCHD? It is certainly true that a tax cut means higher after-tax income on average for all companies, but I don’t expect the benefits to be uniform. In particular, I expect the tax cuts to benefit stocks in the SCHD fund more than the overall market (which, again, is dominated by technology stocks). There are many reasons, and here I will limit myself to the top 2.

Companies that make and sell physical goods tend to have higher effective tax rates. An example is shown below comparing the tax rates of CVX (SCHD’s top holding) and Nvidia (NVDA) (SP500’s top holding). As such, a lower tax rate could lead to a larger impact on CVX than NVDA. Furthermore, energy companies and consumer staples companies often have more of their business and revenue concentrated domestically. This can lead to a more direct impacts when the effective tax rates in the U.S. change. In contrast, multinational tech companies’ tax bills tend to have lower correlation with the U.S. tax rates due to their global operations, various tax strategies, and also the nature of their digital products.

As a final note (and upside for SCHD), the fund currently provides an attractive valuation and also a generous yield to keep investors rewarded while waiting for the above uncertainties to play out. To wit, SCHD's TTM Dividend Yield is 3.7%, which is over three times higher than SPY's 1.09%, as seen. Furthermore, SCHD has also demonstrated a superior dividend growth rate over both the three-year and five-year periods than the SP500. Its three-year dividend growth stands at 8.19% compared to SPY's 6.06%, and its five-year dividend growth is even stronger at 10.87% versus SPY's 4.60%. Finally, note that SCHD’s current TTM yield is about 8% above its four-year average, indicating a slight valuation discount. While in contrast, SPY’s current TTM yield is about 20% below its four-year average, indicating a sizable valuation premium in the broader market.

To close, this SCHD reexamination focused on the developments of the Trump Administration’s economic policies, particularly in the area of tariffs and tax cuts. Unfortunately, I see mixed and even conflicting impacts of these developments on SCHD, resulting in a neutral risk/reward curve and a reiteration of my Hold rating.

data.title: Beyond Meat: Bankruptcy Probable In 2027
data.description:
Introduction

Beyond Meat trades for a market cap of just under $200M, at $2.53 a share, yet has a high chance of leaving equity investors with nothing in the event of a bankruptcy. After a disastrous quarter two earnings report, in which the company reported a nearly 20% drop in sales YoY, Beyond Meat retraced over 13%, to a nearly 52-week low of $2.30, before settling higher on the week at the aforementioned $2.53.

The question remains as to why a company with a high chance of bankruptcy is trading for a valuation above that which most investors deem fair. I believe the answer lies in the high short interest and cost to borrow (nearly 50% annualized), both of which support my statement that the price is well above investors' perceived fair value and propped up by the high cost investors who short the stock must pay to borrow it.

The financials of the company are quite poor. There's really no way around that. It's (in my view) the "low-hanging fruit" of the stock market right now, with a short position having a high probability of profit, if not for that pesky borrowing rate.

Beyond Meat's creditors also believe that the company has a poor chance of recovery, with bonds trading for just 8% of their face value, meaning that Beyond Meat's $1.15B of debt is available to purchase for just $92M. Clearly, the bond market doesn't anticipate their money coming back to them. This sentiment is well justified by their nearly 2x debt/assets ratio and high cash burn.

However, Beyond Meat has a real threat coming in the next ~2.5 months. This threat is the dwindling cash pile compared to their cash burn. In my opinion, this justifies a much larger drop than the one seen post Q2 earnings and is likely to be realized over the next quarter.

That threat of running out of cash is simple, but the impact of the actions taken to solve it will likely be disastrous for the stock price.

Prior Coverage

I have now covered Beyond Meat twice before, over the course of over a year, highlighting the fact that Beyond Meat seems to have no way to pay the amount of debt they issued and that a restructuring is likely.

Beyond Meat remains, in my view, the "low-hanging fruit" of the opportunities out there for short sellers and still remains seen as such by the market, with short interest being significantly higher than that of a typical stock.

This does not, and likely will not, turn into a success or turnaround story. Many holders in Beyond Meat are retail, as shown by the various pumps in the stock price, and many believe that the business can turn around. I disagree. It is clear to me that Beyond Meat cannot survive the next two years.

If its net margin became 100% and it maintained its revenue, it still could not afford to pay its debt off by 2027.

The only way that Beyond Meat, without a sudden jump in revenue and near 100% net margin, can afford to pay off its $1.15B in debt coming due in March of '27 is if it secures further financing to the tune of over 6x its market cap.

I will likely continue to cover Beyond Meat and maintain a sizable short call position in my portfolio.

Beyond Meat's Financials

Metric Q2 YoY % Change
Revenue -19.6%
Gross Profit -37.2%
Net Income +15.2% (was -$35M, now -$30M)

Interest expense on debt

 +100%

Cash and Equivalents

 -29%

Summarized, the company can be said to be in a bit of a conundrum, taking on new debt to simply continue operating... at a loss.

On June 26th, Beyond Meat filed an 8-K, stating that they found new financing, up to $100M at 12% annualized interest, due 2030. Beyond Meat has the option to extend this to 2035; however, the interest rate would jump to 17.5% per the terms of the agreement.

Per the 8-K, "On June 26, 2025, at the Company’s request, Unprocessed Foods, as the sole Lender at such time, made a Delayed Draw Term Loan to the Company in the principal amount of $40.0 million." This adds 4.8M annually to their interest expense, roughly doubling it, and creates a $0.115/share interest expense on the company. This is not negligible, representing almost 5% of the company's market cap.

Discussing revenue, the company's inability to even maintain revenue is extremely concerning. Due to Beyond Meat's lack of any serious intellectual property protections over any of their core products, a rise in the number of competitors, and the lack of growth in product popularity necessary to sustain revenue, Beyond Meat has seen a nearly 33% reduction in revenue since 2021, with revenues shrinking nearly ~20% YoY a quarter in two of the last 8 reported earnings and over 7% YoY on 6 of the last 8 earnings.

The drop in revenue hasn't had too significant an impact on gross profit, as their COGS appear to be fairly fixed, hovering around $60M/quarter for the last 6 quarters. Beyond Meat still has a positive gross margin, with $34M reported in TTM gross profit, which is promptly wiped out by operating expenses, consistently driving operating income below -$30M.

Beyond Meat burns through a fairly consistent ~$25-35M of cash on a quarterly basis, only getting worse over the last year, with TTM free cash flow being -$125M. This cash burn has reduced their cash pile by 30% YoY, from $145M to just over $104M.

Beyond Meat's Cash Burn and Debt

As mentioned above, Beyond Meat burns through cash with a remarkable consistency and has only kept its head above the $100M waterline by issuing $40M in debt this most recent quarter (Q2'25) at a 12% annualized rate. This, paired with their $47M share issuance in Q4 of 2024, has kept them afloat, although, as highlighted by the reduction in cash, they are still not doing swimmingly.

This new debt, at a 12% annualized rate, adds a significant amount of interest to their income statement, and if they don't want shareholders to see a rapid decline in cash, it will only get worse as they borrow the other $60M. At their current burn rate, I foresee the remainder of the $100M being borrowed in 2025, bringing their total annualized interest expense up to $16M, or over 15% of their current cash pile. While their debt is zero-coupon, it still poses an issue for reported earnings and in my opinion, makes the case for bankruptcy/restructuring discussed in the next section even more of a "slam dunk."

Beyond Meat has over $1.28B in debt, up from $1.22B, due to the $40M borrowing and a $12M jump in lease obligations due to their capitalization. This is a huge problem for Beyond Meat, and management is aware of this. On August 12th, John Boken of AlixPartners filed his Form 3, the initial filing of a director. Brought in to run "damage control" as the "interim Chief Transformation Officer," Boken is likely to focus on a reduction in costs and securing future financing to maintain control over the company for as long as possible.

The bulk of Beyond Meat's debt comes due in March of 2027, with $1.15B to be paid out at once, a task that is far outside of the company's current capabilities. A failure to make this payment would constitute a default.

The Tools In Management's Toolbox; Both of Which Hurt.

In order to maintain the cash pile necessary to avoid restructuring, management has two tools, one of which hurts more in the short run and the other in the long run.

Beyond Meat has a shelf offering for up to $200M of stock, allowing it to issue stock onto the market at any time without further approval (of which ~$40M was issued in Q4'24). Any indication of selling would harm the stock significantly, as the average monthly volume is only ~80M shares, or (at current price) ~$200M.

That's right. Almost the entire monthly trading value of Beyond Meat is at the disposal of the board to issue at any time. This fact alone should immediately blacklist Beyond Meat from any serious consideration for investment; however, it's not even as bad as the other option when it comes to long-term harm to shareholder value.

The other option, which is more likely to be exhausted first, is to continue the borrowing of debt at absurd rates. This option seems like the obvious choice, especially when raising the equivalent in stock would kill the share price; however, this will prove disastrous for shareholders and bondholders in the long run unless they can become profitable, as it further reduces shareholder and creditor equity.

Both tools management can use are clearly sledgehammers to capital structure when what's needed is a scalpel to their margins. As discussed in the Risks section of this article, the only meaningful threat I see to a short position is a pivot in their profitability; however, even that would need a significant expansion in revenue to be a large enough shovel to dig out of the hole their debt has placed them in.

Management Activity

5 directors filed Form 4, all disposing of stock, on September 4th of 2025. Clearly, insiders aren't rushing to buy more at this price and are taking advantage of the post-earnings window to dispose of stock.

Management's addition of Boken to the board indicates they are taking drastic measures to ensure a turnaround and to avoid restructuring for as long as possible.

Management is acutely aware of the issues they face and (as shown by how they have kept the company alive for a considerable time, considering the long-term debt they have) have maintained strong current assets (including cash) relative to their short-term liabilities.

Suggested Trade and Risk

Beyond Meat has had sharp rallies before, just this year having risen 110% from all-time lows to a price of $4.80 a share in July; however, the fundamentals are not kind to the concept of maintaining a strong valuation over the long term.

With time working against Beyond Meat, sharp rallies in the stock price pose a risk in the short term, but a risk that should still be addressed. Beyond Meat has options, which can be used to hedge a short position, but the selling of call spreads or purchase of deep in-the-money put options to simulate a short position without the undefined risk potential is (in my opinion) the way to go.

Both of these options also have the benefit of avoiding explicitly paying the cost to borrow (and while it's admittedly baked into the premium skew on the options chain,) allowing investors to minimize and retain predictability of the premiums collected and paid.

The risks of this trade involve the aforementioned sharp upward swing in Beyond Meat's stock price due to a "meme rally" or sudden influx of capital, but without an extreme swing in the company's profitability or news indicative of a favorable buyout, I struggle to see a future in which Beyond Meat's stock doesn't continue to decline.

As always, the risk with naked short selling is undefined, potentially exposing investors to unlimited downside, but with a deep in-the-money put or a short ATM call spread, you can ensure that your risk is capped.

I don't see Beyond Meat's positive gross margin as being a risk, due to the consistent nature of their negative operating margin and the large amount of debt that comes due in 2027.

data.title: CAVA Group: A Rare Opportunity To Buy The Dip
data.description:
Just because the stock market is soaring to new heights doesn't mean that the macroeconomy is rallying as well. On the contrary, consumer-facing businesses have been flashing warning signs on consumer confidence and spending for several quarters, and nowhere is this more keenly felt than in the restaurant sector, where same-restaurant sales growth has been declining.

For a while, it seemed that fast-growing CAVA Group (NYSE:CAVA) would be more immune to the malaise being felt across the industry, but the Mediterranean chain (frequently described as the new Chipotle (CMG) for Mediterranean bowls) recently shattered investors' hopes by lowering its sales expectations alongside its recent Q2 earnings results. The stock is now down ~45% since the start of January and has lost more than half of its value relative to peaks above $140 notched just earlier this year in February. Is CAVA undergoing a permanent correction, or is there value in a rebound here?

I last wrote a buy article on CAVA in June, when the stock was still trading at $80 per share. At the time, I championed CAVA's rare double-digit same-store sales growth. I'll admit that I was also disappointed by CAVA's Q2 results, but I'm comforted by two factors. First, CAVA is a victim of broader industry weakness, and its peers are doing much worse, suggesting that CAVA is actually gaining, not losing, market share in the fast-casual dining space. Second, we now have a lower valuation entry point for CAVA to compensate for the risks of near-term performance decline. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my "Buy" opinion on CAVA.

Let's discuss the elephant in the room first: CAVA's guidance drop and how the stock's valuation multiples look after the cut. At current share prices near $64, CAVA trades at a market cap of $7.39 billion. Netting off the $290.2 million of cash on CAVA's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $7.10 billion.

Meanwhile, as shown in the chart above, CAVA has cut its full-year same-restaurant sales growth expectations to 4%-6% growth, two points lower than the 6%-8% growth in its prior outlook. We do appreciate, however, that CAVA has held its full-year adjusted EBITDA at $152–$159 million, with the company expecting to slightly accelerate its pace of restaurant expansion to make up for weaker same-restaurant sales growth.

Meanwhile, for FY 2026, Wall Street analysts are expecting CAVA to hit $1.44 billion in revenue, or 22% y/y growth. This effectively relies on CAVA continuing its current high-teens pace of unit expansion, plus single-digit same-restaurant sales growth. If we assume a 15% adjusted EBITDA margin on this revenue profile (flat to Q2 actuals and indicating ~1 point of y/y expansion from H1 2025 actual margins of 14.2%, in line with the company's current pace of margin expansion), adjusted EBITDA next year would be $211.5 million (+36% y/y). This positions CAVA's valuation multiples at:

  • 45x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA
  • 34x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

There's no doubt that CAVA is still an expensive growth stock, trading at ~35x forward adjusted EBITDA versus Chipotle (CMG) in the low 20s. But at the same time, we note that the company's same-restaurant sales growth, though weakening in a tough macroeconomy, still sails well above its peers. The company has additional opportunities to unlock higher diner frequency with the broad rollout of its CAVA rewards program. At the same time, the company is driving double-digit adjusted EBITDA expansion as it gains economies of scale on its corporate operations and continues to keep food and labor costs in check.

I'm staying long here. In my view, CAVA has a tremendous opportunity to increase its store count in the United States as it works with plenty of white space opportunity in an underserved Mediterranean food category.

Q2 Recap

While CAVA's latest results certainly weren't perfect, we note that CAVA retains the distinction of being one of few restaurant stocks to post positive comp sales in the quarter amid macroeconomic deterioration. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

CAVA's revenue grew 20% y/y to $280.6 million, which missed Wall Street's expectations of $285.2 million (+22% y/y) by a 2-point margin. The company opened 16 net-new restaurants in the quarter to end with a total of 398 locations, up 17% y/y.

Of course, what investors were laser-focused on was the company's pace of comp sales growth. CAVA had been an extreme outlier in the fast-casual restaurant space in consistently turning out double-digit SRS trends (which, as a reminder, measures the revenue growth from existing locations and excludes the contribution of newly opened or newly closed stores) - a streak that ended in Q2.

As shown above, SRS decelerated 870 bps sequentially to 2.1%, from 10.8% in the previous quarter. On a three-year stack basis, the company's same restaurant sales growth was 34.7% higher, also decelerating 620 bps sequentially versus Q1. As previously mentioned, this downtrend prompted the company to cut full-year comp sales expectations by 2 points.

That being said, we note that most of CAVA's peers are performing much worse, which is why I tend to think of Q2's stumbles as more of a temporary industry retrenchment rather than a company-specific execution flaw. As shown in the chart below, Chipotle - often considered a bellwether of the restaurant and fast-casual sector - has seen two straight quarters of comp sales declines, recently decelerating 360 bps to -4.0%.

Meanwhile, fellow high-growth brand Sweetgreen has also seen two straight quarters of negative comp sales, falling to -7.6% in Q2 (decelerating 450 bps versus a -3.1% decline in Q1 and much worse than positive 4% growth in Q4).

The only other major fast-casual brand that is holding up relatively well is Shake Shack, which posted 2% comps growth in Q2, but at the same time, traffic has been negative for three straight quarters, as the growth was driven primarily by price jumps.

Relatively speaking, CAVA is still in a healthy position. We think as well that the company's nationwide launch of CAVA Rewards (in October 2024), plus its broadening footprint across the U.S., gives the company plenty of opportunity to freshly engage customers and re-accelerate comp sales. We think that CAVA benefits from secular tailwinds shifting toward healthy eating, and CAVA offers undeniable value, with the opportunity to stuff your bowl with a variety of unlimited greens. The company is also experimenting with new protein options to keep its menu fresh, recently introducing an all-white chicken shawarma option that has seen positive early test results.

We also note, encouragingly, that CFO Trish Bolivar remarked on the Q2 earnings call that same-restaurant sales growth accelerated exiting Q2, suggesting that Q3 may see sequential improvement:

Despite the macroeconomic pressures impacting the broader industry, pressures to which we are not immune, we entered the second quarter with strong same-restaurant sales momentum in line with the guidance we provided in the first quarter. However, as we moved through June, we saw a deceleration in same-restaurant sales driven in part by the timing of our steak launch last year, a protein that filled a meaningful gap on our menu. At the same time, we saw a honeymoon effect from our 2024 new restaurant class, a dynamic we have not experienced before, which significantly outperformed expectations opening at higher-than-anticipated sales volumes. In fact, the strength of this class has driven year 1 cash-on-cash returns above 40%, already meeting our year 2 expectations.

Same-restaurant sales regained momentum in the latter part of the quarter, reaccelerating as we exited Q2 and continued into Q3, and we are encouraged by the sequential improvement. As Brett mentioned, our 2025 openings are also exceeding expectations, trending above $3 million in first year average unit volumes compared to our $2.3 million target, with new restaurant productivity of roughly 109%, further demonstrating the strength and proven portability of our operating model.

The company has also maintained a healthy pace of margin expansion. Restaurant-level operating margins (an industry measure of profitability that excludes corporate overhead) expanded 120 bps y/y to 26.3%, driven by slight savings on labor and rent costs. Adjusted EBITDA in Q2 grew 23% y/y to $42.1 million, with a 15% adjusted EBITDA margin that improved 30 bps y/y.

Key Takeaways

I view CAVA as an innovator and category leader in a Mediterranean food category that is growing in popularity and yet remains quite under-penetrated in the United States. Despite macroeconomic pressures, CAVA is pulling ahead with stronger same-restaurant sales growth than core peers like Chipotle, justifying its valuation premium (near-term valuation multiples are, in any case, an unreliable metric to judge CAVA's potential, given its current pace of top-line and adjusted EBITDA growth). Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Latham Group: After A Surge, Better Opportunities Exist
data.description:
The last few months have been a phenomenal time for shareholders of Latham Group (NASDAQ:SWIM). As an example, back in the middle of June of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a "Hold" candidate. My view at the time was that there was a lot of near-term uncertainty regarding the firm's outlook and a lack of clarity regarding future growth timelines. The near-term issues were centered around broader economic concerns. However, the market seems to have shrugged those off even as the United States appears to be barreling toward a recession. Since publishing that article, the stock is up 42.7%, significantly outperforming the S&P 500's 8.2% rise.

Looking at the data today, I see that the firm posted some rather solid results for its latest fiscal quarter. This and management's decision to reaffirm guidance for 2025 as a whole can be thanked for this recent surge in share price. But honestly, the stock is getting rather lofty. I wouldn't say it's time to downgrade it at this moment. After all, I would say it is around the midpoint of the range of what I would consider to be fair value. However, I certainly don't think the company deserves to continue outperforming the market like it has lately.

Some Great Times… For Now

The only new data that investors have regarding Latham Group covers through the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for SWIM came in at $172.6 million. That represents an increase of 7.8% compared to the $160.1 million that the company reported the previous year. The firm, which operates as the largest producer and marketer of in-ground residential swimming pools in North America, Australia, and New Zealand, said that this rise in revenue was thanks to a $12 million benefit that the company saw from higher sales volumes. It also benefited to the tune of $0.5 million in the form of higher pricing on its products.

In terms of volume growth, some of this was driven by acquisition activities. For instance, in August 2024, management completed the firm’s acquisition of Coverstar Central, its exclusive dealer of automatic safety covers with operations spread across 29 states. The firm followed this purchase up with acquisitions of two of its smaller auto cover dealers in the first half of this year. Management did not break out how much of the revenue growth on a year-over-year basis was driven by organic means compared to acquisition activities. But we do know that, when it comes to covers, the benefit there was $11.7 million compared to only $3.1 million for liners. So I would venture to say that acquisition activities accounted for most of this expansion.

With revenue rising, profitability for the company also improved. Net income grew from $13.3 million to $16 million. Of course, there are other profitability metrics that we should be paying attention to as well. Operating cash flow is one such example. And year over year, it plummeted from $52.4 million to $36 million. But if we strip out changes in working capital, we get more than a doubling from $29.3 million to $60.5 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $34.5 million to $39.9 million.

Thanks to this strong growth for the latest quarter, results for the first half of 2025 ended up coming in quite a bit stronger than the same time in 2024. Revenue of $284.1 million ended up being 4.9% above the $270.8 million reported the previous year. The company benefited to the tune of $14.4 million from higher sales volumes. Unfortunately, lower pricing offset this to some extent by $1.1 million. And as was the case with the second quarter on its own, the revenue growth was driven primarily by increased cover sales. Net profits almost doubled from $5.4 million to $10 million. We did see operating cash flow plummet from $17.9 million to negative $10.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $36.1 million to $66.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $46.8 million to $51 million.

Long term, SWIM should do just fine. The business has a couple of catalysts that could push revenue, profits, and cash flows higher as time goes on. The biggest one of these is the fact that the business has a 50% market share in the fiberglass in-ground pool category. This is a segment of the in-ground swimming pool market that is growing rapidly. In fact, from 2019 through 2024, the share of in-ground pools that are fiberglass has grown from 17% of those installed annually to 23%. And when it comes to its own in-ground swimming pools, 75% are fiberglass. Another positive would be the significant amount of exposure the company has to liners and covers. Between 100,000 and 120,000 all-season covers are replaced every year in the United States. Another 180,000 to 200,000 vinyl liners are replaced annually. This creates a great recurring revenue stream for the company. And since 49% of its overall revenue involves these types of products, it has baked-in staying power.

We also need to consider the fact that management has a very specific plan for how to capture additional growth. For instance, the company continues to focus on expanding its market share in some of the most attractive states for swimming pools. These are what it calls the ‘Sand States’ of Florida, Texas, Arizona, and California. These four states account for roughly two-thirds of all new pool installations in the United States each year. So clearly, they offer a lot of upside for the company.

In these states, management focuses not only on buying up other firms and expanding organically in them; it is focused on doing so in a way that allows it to target master-planned communities. The firm has identified these as a huge opportunity, with around 30,000 new homes constructed in them in 2023 alone. And at this time, 75% of the largest master-planned communities are found in Florida and Texas.

In the near term, however, we could face some issues. Even though management expects revenue growth, using the midpoint of guidance of 8% this year, with sales forecasted to come in at between $535 million and $565 million, actual industry sales in the swimming pool market are forecasted to be flat. High interest rates and the inflationary pressures that the interest rates are aimed at combating are one challenge. But now, with the U.S. economy moving toward a recession, that could lead to additional pain for the company. Even with this being the case, however, management still believes that the business can grow to $750 million in annual revenue at some point in the future. But unfortunately, they have not given a timeline as to when this will be.

Based on management's own estimates for 2025, EBITDA should come in somewhere between $90 million and $100 million. This should translate to an adjusted operating cash flow of about $60.2 million. If we look out into the future, using the same figures that management has provided as a projection, then at some point, the company should be generating around $160 million worth of EBITDA each year, which I believe would translate to adjusted operating cash flow of about $101.4 million.

Using these estimates and using the historical results that the business saw for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently valued. These are multiples that, honestly, are certainly in the fair value range. If only we had a better idea as to what the timeline would be for the future, I might be feeling a bit more optimistic. And this is because, while shares of the company look cheap on that basis, we don't know how many years it will take to get to that point. If it's in two years, for instance, shares would be significantly undervalued. But if it's in 10 years, it's an underwhelming prospect. But I digress. In the chart below, I compared its trading multiples estimated for 2025 to the trading multiples of two other swimming pool-oriented enterprises. What I found was that, on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, Latham Group is in the middle of the group. However, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, it is quite a bit cheaper than the other players. This alone could justify some upside. But for me, it's not enough to become bullish on.

Takeaway

At this point, Latham Group seems to be doing quite well for itself. Honestly, I am a bit surprised by how robust financial results have been considering the state of the economy. High interest rates and inflationary pressures are not normally a recipe for success when it comes to higher-cost items like swimming pools. The recurring nature of some of its revenue certainly has proven to be beneficial during this time. But even with that factored in, Latham Group appears to be doing quite well. But as a value investor who looks for true bargains, the surge that the stock has experienced has pushed the price higher than what I feel comfortable with. To be clear, the stock is not expensive. However, for someone who prioritizes a large margin of safety when making investment decisions, I just don't see that here. Because of this, I believe that keeping the firm rated a "Hold" is the right choice.

data.title: The State Of The Consumer: What Dollar Tree's Q2 Signals
data.description:
I enjoy reading company earnings not just for the numbers, but for what they reveal about the state of the economy. Dollar Tree (NASDAQ:DLTR) is one of the most interesting in that regard because its customer base cuts across income levels and its trends often signal how Americans are adjusting to shifting economic realities. The company’s latest Q2 results were solid, but the real story is not in the headline EPS beat or the revenue line. It is in the way people are spending, how those behaviors are shifting, and what that says about the broader consumer backdrop.

With inflation still stubborn and tariffs threatening to push prices higher, households are reassessing how they allocate their budgets. That pressure creates an opportunity for Dollar Tree. The retailer is not only holding on to its value-seeking customers, but it is also drawing in a new group of higher-income shoppers who are choosing to spend more deliberately. This is no longer just a trade-down narrative—it is also a trade-in story, one that shows how Dollar Tree is capturing both sides of the consumer spectrum.

Higher Income Consumers Are Coming and Staying

It is impressive that Dollar Tree added 2.4 million active shoppers in the past year, and two-thirds of them earn more than $100,000 annually, a clear sign that the brand is breaking into higher-income households that historically were not its base. These are not casual visits either, as the share of customers shopping three or more times a month increased by 11% last quarter, pointing to a structural shift in shopping patterns. Expanded product variety, a wider mix of price points, and stronger merchandising are encouraging repeat visits and broadening the store’s appeal. The definition of value is moving beyond low-income budgets to include how consumers across income levels perceive convenience, assortment, and savings in a tighter spending environment.

As of the end of Q2, we have added 2.4 million new customers on a last 12 months basis, consistent with our pace in recent quarters. And nearly 2/3 of those new customers came from households earning $100,000 or more. – Mike Creedon, CEO

Core Shoppers Are Holding the Line

Even as Dollar Tree expands its reach into higher-income households, it has not ceded ground on its core customers, the lower-income customers who have long been the backbone of the business. It is notable that among these consumers, order sizes remain consistent, and management emphasized that unit growth is still positive despite price adjustments, reinforcing the perception of value among its most price-sensitive shoppers. This loyalty is especially important at a time when other retailers are reporting weakness in their low-income segments, and it gives Dollar Tree a level of stability that supports both revenue visibility and earnings durability. The combination of a resilient core and incremental higher-income growth provides a balanced base that strengthens the company’s outlook.

But what was interesting was we still saw very strong performance from our lower income customer. So really, the pack sizes that we have, the ability to help them kind of stretch their budget and make it to that next paycheck, we saw that all strong – Mike Creedon, CEO

Management Is Leaning In, But Staying Realistic

We are now headed into the busy holiday period, and with the overhang of trade wars and tariffs, retailers across the board have plenty of reasons to be cautious. The questions are the same for everyone: will the consumer prove resilient, should more of the tariff costs be passed on through pricing, will volumes take a hit if prices move up, and how much pressure will margins face if they do not? Dollar Tree is no different, and management acknowledged that it is grappling with the same uncertainties.

What Dollar Tree has done differently is lay out a clear plan for dealing with the uncertainty. Management talked about five main areas they are focusing on: keeping pricing disciplined, changing some of their sourcing, being more selective with the products they carry, working suppliers harder on negotiations, and raising prices in certain spots where it makes sense. It is not a perfect solution, but it shows they are thinking carefully about how to balance the risks and the trade-offs rather than pretending the challenges are not there.

We love the traffic we're seeing, the ticket -- I mean, that discretionary comp in Q2 is absolutely incredible. So we're very pleased with how our business is responding. But if you look at the lower income consumer and you look at the challenges that they're facing every day just across their entire life, in terms of what things cost, it's just -- it's a cause for caution on our part. We think it's the right posture. – Mike Creedon, CEO

Bottom Line: A Selective, Not Weak, Consumer

In sum, the most important point is that consumers are still spending, but the way they approach their purchases has shifted. Shoppers are becoming more selective about where and how they allocate their budgets, and Dollar Tree is emerging as a clear beneficiary of that shift. For those who need to stretch every dollar, it remains a destination for value and consistency, while at the same time it is increasingly attracting higher-income households who are looking to spend more deliberately. The core base is holding steady, the higher-income cohort is shopping more frequently, and management retains flexibility through pricing and merchandising levers. In a retail landscape where many peers are still struggling to define who their core customer really is, Dollar Tree has managed to secure loyalty from both ends of the spectrum, and that balance is proving to be a competitive advantage.

data.title: SPLV: Everything You Need To Know About This Low-Volatility ETF
data.description:
The Invesco S&P 500 Low Volatility ETF (NYSEARCA:SPLV) is a well-established fund which aims to offer investors the upside of the U.S. equity market with a smoother return profile.

The traditional thinking would be that more risk equals higher returns, as would follow from the efficient markets hypothesis. If risk is priced correctly, then shouldering more of it should offer greater gains in compensation. But there may be a low-volatility anomaly.

Historically, there has been some evidence that higher volatility as a factor does not necessarily add to returns. As far back as 1972, economists Robert Haugen and James Heins suggested the opposite, that low volatility stocks may provide greater average returns than their riskier counterparts. In fact, a 2015 analysis of prior financial shocks found that low-volatility delivers thanks to a paradox where you can gain more by losing less.

This theory is plausible because higher realized price volatility is often associated with cyclical companies or companies which have uncertain business models. A biotech company, for example could see huge gains or be nearly wiped out depending on the results of a critical drug trial. A technology company could soar or be made obsolete depending on how consumers react to an upcoming new product launch cycle. Whereas a telecom, utility, or packaged foods company is unlikely to see its value skyrocket or collapse in any given year. Over time, a portfolio of these more durable types of businesses could outperform a portfolio with a more boom and bust nature.

Invesco launched SPLV back in May 2011. And for quite a few years, it appeared that the fund was successfully capturing the so-called low-volatility anomaly. Between inception and the onset of the pandemic in 2020, SPLV had slightly outperformed the S&P 500 on a total return basis while having significantly lower beta as compared to the overall market. Equal or slightly higher returns with less downside risk would be a great outcome.

Over the past five years, however, the return profile has turned much less favorable. Here is a chart showing the SPLV fund's returns compared to the S&P 500 since inception. You can see both the strong showing up through March 2020 and the much less compelling return profile since then:

To be fair, the SPLV has continued to deliver gains at a fairly linear pace, with the chart following the same general trajectory that it has since the fund's inception. However, the overall S&P 500 has seen its return profile markedly accelerate to the upside in recent years while SPLV has not followed suit.

A positive case for SPLV could be that the overall U.S. equity market has gotten frothy and that gains have been fairly concentrated in a handful of leading technology companies such as the so-called Magnificent 7 technology firms. If that trend reversed course, the S&P 500 could underperform going forward while at the same time safe haven low-volatility stocks could be in for a period of significant outperformance.

How Is SPLV Constructed?

Low-volatility sounds great in theory, but the concept can mean different things to different people. Index construction is an important part in trying to harness the potential of this strategy and turning it into actual realized returns. So, let's take a look at how SPLV is put together.

Invesco bases its SPLV fund on the underlying S&P 500 Low Volatility Index. This index starts out with the investable universe of the S&P 500. Out of the 500 companies, this index picks out the 100 of those that had the lowest realized volatility over the past year (past 252 trading days, specifically).

The index sizes these positions in inverse proportion to each stock's volatility, meaning that the absolute lowest volatility companies within the S&P 500 receive the largest weight in the ETF. That said, in practice, the actual weighting does not end up too different from what you'd get with equal weighting. As of this writing, no position makes up more than 1.3% of SPLV's portfolio, and the top 10 holdings account for just 11.7% of the index. The index is rebalanced quarterly; these occur in February, May, August, and November.

Broadly speaking, SPLV ends up giving investors relatively close to equal-weighted exposure to the lowest quintile of the S&P 500 in terms of realized volatility over the prior year.

Let's see how this portfolio construction strategy plays out on a sectoral basis. Here are the ETF's weightings by sector as of this writing:

  • Utilities: 21.4%
  • Financials: 15.7%
  • Consumer Defensive: 15.6%
  • Real Estate: 9.9%
  • Health Care: 8.9%
  • Consumer Cyclical: 8.9%
  • Industrials: 7.9%
  • Technology: 6.7%
  • Communication: 2.8%
  • Basic Materials: 2.1%

As you might expect, this ETF has a larger exposure to certain sectors which tend to be more predictable and have steady earnings and cash flows. SPLV has a far greater exposure to these sectors, like utilities and consumer defensive, than the S&P 500. Meanwhile, SPLV has less involvement in faster-moving sectors. Notably, technology has just a 6.7% weighting in SPLV as of September 2025, compared to a 34.7% weighting for technology in the S&P 500.

It seems that traditionally low-volatility strategies have been able to outperform and show strong factor performance because there's been a lot of dispersion throughout the S&P 500 and overall stock market. There would be leaders and laggards within various sectors and there'd be room for the steadier blue-chip names to deliver alpha compared to the overall index.

Over the past few years, however, that edge has seemingly faded. The returns from the large-cap tech stocks have been strong enough so as to give a sizable advantage to growth and momentum-based factor strategies rather than low-volatility. It remains to be seen if market conditions will revert to their prior norms, or if the concentration of market capitalization and profitability within a handful of gigantic tech names has permanently weakened the low-volatility edge, at least as it relates to large-cap U.S. equities.

On the other hand, the low volatility equity basket has still continued to produce decent returns. SPLV is up 54%, including dividends, over the past five years. That's not a bad return, especially for a lower beta product. However, it hasn't come anywhere near keeping pace with the overall U.S. equity bull market.

If you think the S&P 500 is set for a pause or a significant pullback, however, this could be an interesting time to give low-volatility strategies a fresh look.

SPLV Versus Peer Funds

So, how does this ETF stack up to the competition? SPLV's closest peer fund is probably the iShares MSCI USA Min Vol Factor ETF (USMV). That iShares fund has $23 billion in assets under management and has also been in business since 2011.

USMV has the same general investment goal as SPLV, which is to lower portfolio volatility while maintaining strong returns from owning large-cap U.S. equities.

However, USMV's weighting methodology is significantly different. USMV has 176 holdings, giving it wider exposure than SPLV. It also has a much different composition on a sectoral basis, with USMV having 29.8% of its holdings in technology as compared to just 6.7% of USMV's holdings.

USMV's different sector composition has led to fairly significant outperformance over the past decade as compared to SPLV. Of the two, SPLV performed better during the 2022 market drawdown but has otherwise struggled to keep up with its leading low-volatility competitor:

As a final note, USMV has a 15 basis point expense ratio, which is a fair bit lower than SPLV's 25 basis point annual fee. Both of those fees are reasonable, but USMV's is a bit more investor friendly. If the technology sector starts to underperform going forward, it could set up another period - like 2022 - where SPLV delivers superior returns. Otherwise, however, USMV may continue to attract a larger share of investor funds going into the low volatility space.

Those are two of the most well-known large-cap low volatility ETFs. However, there are other flavors of low-volatility funds available as well. Some of these would include the Invesco S&P MidCap Low Volatility ETF (XMLV), Invesco S&P 500 High Dividend Low Volatility ETF (SPHD), and the iShares MSCI EAFE Min Vol Factor ETF (EFAV).

SPLV Bottom Line

Admittedly, low volatility methodologies have lagged against the overall U.S. stock market in recent years. But there is still reason to think that the underlying thesis underpinning the strategy still has merit.

To make that case from another perspective, consider the following. If a portfolio falls 20%, it needs to go up by 25% to break even overall. If a portfolio falls 50%, it needs to go up 100% to get back to breakeven. And if a portfolio slumps 70%, it would need to go up by 233% merely to get back to the original starting point.

This line of thinking shows the merit behind a fund like SPLV. If an investor can avoid suffering those large drawdowns in the first place, the gains tend to take care of themselves. In theory, a low-vol fund like SPLV should suffer a much smaller drawdown in a bear market and thus make it far easier to bounce back when the cycle turns.

The U.S. stock market has been in an upward trend for many years now. Investors, by and large, are not thinking about avoiding drawdowns at the moment; rather, the attention is on maximizing gains.

However, sooner or later, another bear market will arrive. For investors that are worried about valuations in the market today and want to minimize risk going forward, a fund like SPLV could be a prudent allocation at this time.

SPLV should be generally suitable for many types of investors. The fund is primarily aimed at delivering long-term capital gains, though there is a decent income component as well. The 1.8% dividend yield for SPLV is significantly higher than the 1.1% yield on the S&P 500 or the 0.5% yield on offer from the Nasdaq 100 as of this writing. The higher dividend payout could merit sticking shares in a tax-sheltered account.

Low-volatility strategies are likely to come back into favor again at some point. The methodology around avoiding big market drawdowns and benefitting from the stability of blue-chip companies should still work going forward, despite the recent trend of underperformance.

That said, there are a lot of low volatility funds out there, and they take meaningfully different approaches to their portfolio composition. USMV, with its more tech-centric method of constructing its low-vol fund, has had a better run recently. But if the market overall and tech in particular face a correction, SPLV might deliver alpha for its shareholders once again.
